§6Long-Lived Nuclear Waste§f

§aTransuranic waste with long half-lives — process it for plutonium and rare isotopes!§f

§bLong-Lived Nuclear Waste§f is a class of waste with a long average half-life, usually because the constituent radioisotopes are transuranic elements or neutron capture products. There are §e5 sub-classes§f: Th-232, U-233, U-235, Np-237, and Sa-326 (schrabidium).

§6Production§f: Extracted from spent fuel using a §bSILEX§f. Each fuel type yields its corresponding waste class.

§6Non-Decayed Recycling§f:
§b• Thorium-232§f → §e40% U-233§f, §e35% U-235§f, §e25% generic waste
§b• Uranium-233§f → §e15% U-235§f, §e25% Np-237§f, §e45% Pu-239§f, §e15% Tc-99§f
§b• Uranium-235§f → §e25% Np-237§f, §e45% Pu-239§f, §e15% Pu-240§f, §e15% Tc-99§f
§b• Neptunium-237§f → §e15% U-238§f, §e40% Pu-239§f, §e15% Pu-240§f, §e15% Tc-99§f, §e15% generic waste
§b• Schrabidium-326§f → §e25% Sa-327§f, §e18% Euphemium§f, §e16% Ghiorsium-336§f, §e8% Tantalium§f, §e8% Neodymium§f, §e25% generic waste

§6Decayed Recycling§f (after §bNuclear Waste Disposal Drum§f):
§b• Th-232§f → §e35% Lead§f, §e40% Bismuth§f, §e15% Dust§f, §e10% generic waste
§b• U-233§f → §e65% Lead§f, §e15% Bismuth§f, §e20% generic waste
§b• Np-237§f → §e16% U-238§f, §e55% Lead§f, §e20% Dust§f, §e9% generic waste
§b• Sa-326§f → similar to non-decayed with less yield

§6Radiation-Powered Engine§f: Long-lived waste can also power a §bRadiation-Powered Engine§f directly, producing energy while decaying.

§eTip§f: Long-Lived Waste is §evaluable§f — it contains high proportions of Pu-239 and U-233 that can be recycled back into fuel!
